name: Amir ibn Fuhayra
title: Amir ibn Fuhayra (RA): The Guide on the Hijrah
meta_description: The story of Amir ibn Fuhayra (RA), a freed slave who guided the Prophet Muhammad on the Hijrah, fought at Badr and Uhud, and died with the words: I have succeeded, by Allah.
keywords: Amir ibn Fuhayra, story of Amir ibn Fuhayra, Hijrah guide, freed slave companion, the cave of Thawr, Bir Ma'una, sahaba stories
lede: Some of the most important moments in early Islam had very few witnesses. The cave on the way out of Makkah held only the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and one friend. The note that turned an armed pursuer into a believer was written by one hand.

This is the story of Amir ibn Fuhayra (may Allah be pleased with him): a man born into slavery who had one of the closest seats in all of history, and who left it with a single sentence on his lips. He is not a famous name, and that is part of why his life is worth telling.

sections.h2: Born into slavery, early to faith
paras: Amir ibn Fuhayra was of Abyssinian descent, and his whole life began in bondage. He was the slave of a man named Tufayl ibn Abdullah al-Azdi. Tufayl was the stepson of Abu Bakr (RA): Abu Bakr had married Umm Ruman, the mother of Aisha (RA), and Tufayl was her son from an earlier marriage. So Amir lived close to the household that would one day shape the whole story.

Amir accepted Islam very early, while he was still a slave. He believed before the Muslims gathered in the house of al-Arqam, which places him among the first of the first, the earliest people on earth to follow the Prophet ﷺ. He had nothing to gain from it. A slave who believed only made his own life harder.

And it did. What is written about Amir is hard to read: there was almost no method of torture his captors did not try on him. Burning coal let to drip down his back, days without sleep, days without water or food, dragging through the streets, chains, and beatings from anyone who cared to. The sources do not even name a single tormentor, because a slave of his standing was fair game for the powerful of Makkah to test in any way they liked.
He carried all of it for a faith he had chosen freely, with no clan to defend him and no fortune to buy him out. That is the man who would soon be trusted with the safety of the Prophet ﷺ.

sections.h2: Bought back, and a new life as a shepherd
paras: Abu Bakr (RA) had a habit that defined him: he spent his wealth buying the freedom of believing slaves who were being tortured for their faith. Freeing captives was already his way before Islam, and now it became a mission. When he saw what Amir was enduring, he purchased his freedom.

Amir was free, but he chose to stay close. He was no longer anyone's slave, yet his loyalty and his love settled on the man who had freed him, and who would not want to stay near a man like Abu Bakr. Knowing Amir's character, and his early place in Islam, the feeling went both ways. He became a shepherd for Abu Bakr, tending his flocks out in the open country around Makkah.

It looked like an ordinary job. In truth it was a gift of provision from Allah, and it would place him, before long, at the center of one of the most dangerous and important journeys in the history of Islam.

sections.h2: The cave, and the sheep that hid their tracks
paras: When the order came to leave Makkah, the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr (RA) hid in a cave for three days and three nights while Quraysh searched for them. We know of Asma bint Abi Bakr (RA), who carried food to the cave, and of her brother Abdullah, who carried news. Amir's role was just as constant.

Each day, at a time when he would not be noticed, Amir brought the flock near the cave. The sheep fed the two men inside. Then he walked the animals back over the path they had taken, so their hooves trampled out the footprints of the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr. Nourishment and concealment, in a single quiet act, repeated day after day, with searchers combing the hills for the very men he was feeding.

When the three days ended, it was Amir who came with the camels for the journey. The Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr, and Amir set out together. Of all the people on earth, he was the one who rode beside the Prophet ﷺ on the migration, never far from him for the length of that dangerous road, and helped guide them toward Madinah.

ayah.text: Even if you do not help the Prophet, God helped him when the disbelievers drove him out: when the two of them were in the cave, he [Muhammad] said to his companion, 'Do not worry, God is with us,' and God sent His calm down to him, aided him with forces invisible to you, and brought down the disbelievers' plan. God's plan is higher: God is almighty and wise.

sections.h2: The note he wrote on the road
paras: On that same journey came the famous moment with Suraqah ibn Malik, who set out to claim the bounty on the Prophet's head. As he closed in, his horse sank into the ground beneath him and threw him, again and again, until he understood he was being held back by something he could not fight.

Suraqah gave up the bounty of this world and asked instead to be counted among the believers. He requested a written promise of safety, and the Prophet ﷺ told him of a day to come, when Suraqah would wear the gold bracelets of the emperor of Persia.

The promise had to be written down, and the one who wrote it was Amir. Think of the view he had: a freed slave, pen in hand, recording the words of the Prophet ﷺ at the very edge of the migration, for a man who had ridden out to kill them. Few people in all of history had a closer seat to events that would be told and retold for centuries.

He had been owned, beaten, and counted as nothing in Makkah. Now he wrote the safe conduct of a former enemy at the word of the Prophet ﷺ. The same person, the same hands, lifted by faith into a place no one would have predicted for him.

sections.h2: A veteran of Badr and Uhud
paras: In Madinah, Amir gained another honor. He fought at Badr, alongside Abu Bakr (RA), and so he counted among the veterans of that first decisive day, a rank the companions treasured for the rest of their lives.

When the trial of Uhud came and many were scattered, Amir was among those who held their ground and did not flee. He had been persecuted for his faith in Makkah, he had made the Hijrah in the closest possible company, and now he stood firm on one of the hardest battlefields the Muslims had yet faced.

His was not a life of a single famous scene. It was faith proven again and again: in the torture cell, on the road out of Makkah, and in the line at Badr and Uhud. Every stage asked more of him, and every stage he gave it.

About three or four months after Uhud, people from Najd asked the Prophet ﷺ to send teachers who could recite the Quran and instruct them in the faith. Amir was literate and among the best of the companions in this, so he was sent with a group of the finest reciters.

It was a betrayal. The party was attacked and killed near the well of Ma'una. Imagine the weight of it. These were among the best of the people the Prophet ﷺ still had with him, sent out in hope to open a new path for the faith, and instead they were murdered. The number of the fallen was close to that of Uhud, where almost every household in Madinah had already lost someone. Now, only months later, the city was struck again. Amir was one of those killed. He was about forty.

The man who killed him, Jabbar ibn Salma, drove a sword clean through his body. As it pierced him, Amir looked up and said, 'Fuztu wallahi.' I have succeeded, by Allah. They were his last words, spoken not in pain but in certainty, with the blade still through him.

Jabbar was shaken by what he had seen in Amir's face and heard in his voice. He went to the Muslims afterward asking what those words could mean, and what he had witnessed led him to accept Islam himself. Amir's final act on this earth was, without intending it, a call to faith.

sections.h2: The death that opened a door
paras: There is a hadith in which the Prophet ﷺ spoke of Allah being pleased with two men, one of whom had killed the other, and yet both ending up in Paradise. Scholars point to moments like this one as a living example of that. The killer is led, through the very act of killing, toward the faith of the one he struck down.

It echoes another famous reversal: Wahshi, who threw the spear that martyred Hamza, and who later became a Muslim. Amir's story belongs in that small company. His murder was not the end of his witness. It was the start of his killer's. What turned Jabbar was not an argument but a certainty he could see, a faith so settled that even a sword could not disturb it.

Some narrations add that Amir's body was raised and never found, so the burial rites could not be carried out for him. There is no strong chain behind that detail, and we mention it only as it has been passed down. What is certain is enough: after persecution, migration, Badr, and Uhud, his last breath was an act of worship and an act of calling others to the truth.

lessons_intro: Amir was never the most famous name in the room. His life is a quiet argument that the people history forgets to count are often the ones holding everything together.

lessons.bold: Faithfulness shows in the unseen work.
lessons.text: Amir's greatest service was walking sheep over footprints so no one would notice. The most important roles are not always the visible ones.
He was freed and owed nothing, yet he stayed close to the man who freed him and served with love. He chose loyalty when he no longer had to.

lessons.bold: Conviction can be felt by others.
lessons.text: The man who killed him became a believer because of what he saw in Amir's certainty. Sincere faith leaves a mark, even on those set against it.

lessons.bold: A whole life can rest on a final word.
lessons.text: After torture, migration, and battle, his last breath carried one clear thought: that he had succeeded. He measured success by what mattered to him most.

faq.a: A companion of Abyssinian descent who was born into slavery and freed by Abu Bakr (RA). He was among the earliest Muslims, served as a shepherd, guided the Prophet ﷺ on the Hijrah, and was later martyred near the well of Ma'una.

faq.q: What was his role in the Hijrah?
faq.a: While the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr (RA) hid in the cave, Amir brought sheep each day to feed them and walked the flock back over their tracks to hide them. After three days he brought the camels and traveled with them as their guide toward Madinah.

As he was killed near the well of Ma'una, he looked up and said, 'Fuztu wallahi,' meaning 'I have succeeded, by Allah.' The man who killed him was so affected by what he witnessed that he later accepted Islam.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Amir ibn Fuhayra?
faq.a: That hidden, faithful service carries real weight, that gratitude can outlast obligation, and that sincere conviction can move even those who oppose it.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of the good we do is never seen. Amir spent his most important days doing work no crowd would ever applaud: feeding two men in a cave, brushing away their footprints, riding quietly beside them. He did not need an audience to do it well, and he did not measure his worth by who noticed. The reward for that kind of work is kept somewhere safer than human memory.

There is a question hidden in his last words too. He said he had succeeded, and he meant it about the only things he had carried with him out of this life: his faith and his sincerity. It is worth asking, in an ordinary day, what we would call success if everything else were stripped away. Amir already knew his answer, and it cost him everything to say it. May our quiet, unseen acts be counted, and may we be given his certainty when it matters most.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Amir ibn Fuhayra (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 9:40). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported, and we have noted where a detail rests on a weak chain.

social.caption:
Some of the most important moments in early Islam had almost no witnesses. The cave on the way out of Makkah held the Prophet ﷺ and one companion. That companion, Amir ibn Fuhayra (may Allah be pleased with him), had been born into slavery and freed by Abu Bakr.

While the two men hid in the cave, Amir brought sheep each day to feed them, then walked the flock back over their footprints so no one could follow. He guided them on the Hijrah, and he wrote the note of safety for the pursuer who gave up the chase.

He died near the well of Ma'una with five words: I have succeeded, by Allah. The man who killed him was so moved that he became a believer.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The man who hid the Prophet's footprints
email.preview: He fed two men in a cave, then walked the sheep back over their tracks so no one could follow.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some of the most important moments in early Islam had almost no witnesses. The cave on the way out of Makkah held the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and one friend. That friend had been born into slavery.

His name was Amir ibn Fuhayra (may Allah be pleased with him). He accepted Islam very early, before the Muslims even gathered in the house of al-Arqam. As a slave with no protection, he was tortured in almost every way his captors could devise. Abu Bakr (RA) bought his freedom, and Amir, owing nothing now, chose to stay close and become his shepherd.

That humble job placed him at the heart of the Hijrah. While the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr hid in the cave for three days, Amir brought the flock near each day so the sheep could feed them. Then he walked the animals back over the path, so their hooves erased the footprints. Food and cover, in one quiet act, day after day.

When the three days ended, he brought the camels and rode out as their guide. On the road, when the pursuer Suraqah gave up the chase and asked for a promise of safety, it was Amir who wrote it down at the Prophet's instruction.

He fought at Badr and held his ground at Uhud. A few months later, he was sent with a group of reciters to teach people who had asked for guidance, and the party was betrayed and killed near the well of Ma'una. As a sword passed through him, Amir looked up and said five words: I have succeeded, by Allah.

The man who killed him could not forget what he had seen in Amir's face. He went to the Muslims asking what those words meant, and what he learned led him to accept Islam.

There is a lesson in Amir that is easy to miss. His greatest service was work no crowd would ever see: feeding two men in a cave, brushing away their tracks. He did it well without an audience, and he died sure he had succeeded.

May Allah be pleased with him, and give us his certainty when it matters most.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Some of the most important moments in early Islam had almost no witnesses. One of the people who was there, again and again, had been born a slave.

Amir ibn Fuhayra (may Allah be pleased with him) accepted Islam very early, while he was still in bondage. A slave who believed had no protection, and he was tortured in almost every way his captors could invent. Then Abu Bakr saw what he was enduring and bought his freedom.
Amir owed him nothing after that. He stayed close anyway, out of love, and became Abu Bakr's shepherd. It looked like an ordinary job. It was about to place him at the center of the Hijrah.

When the Prophet and Abu Bakr hid in the cave, Amir brought the flock near each day. The sheep fed the two men inside. Then he walked the animals back over the same ground, so their hooves erased the footprints. When the three days ended, he came with the camels, and rode out beside the Prophet as the guide of the journey.

On the road, a pursuer named Suraqah gave up the chase and asked instead for a written promise of safety. The hand that wrote it, at the Prophet's instruction, was Amir's. A freed slave, recording the words of the Prophet at the very edge of the migration.

He fought at Badr and stood firm at Uhud. A few months later he was sent with a group of reciters to teach people who had asked for guidance, and near the well of Ma'una they were betrayed and killed.

As the sword passed through him, Amir looked up and said, 'I have succeeded, by Allah.' They were his last words, spoken in certainty, not pain. The man who killed him was so shaken by what he saw that he later became a believer himself.
storybook.reflection: Amir's greatest service was work no one was meant to see: feeding two men in a cave, then hiding their footprints. He never had an audience for the things that mattered most, and he did them well anyway. When the end came, he was sure he had succeeded, and he meant it about the only things he carried with him: his faith and his sincerity. It is worth asking what we would call success if everything else were taken away.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
